---
title: "Group Assignment Causal Machine Learning"
authors: "Egor Trushkov, 6672874; Dominic Pöltl, 6618670"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_notebook:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
---

### 2.1 Literature Review

...

### 2.2 Identification of LATT/LATU

#### LATT

To identify the LATT we use the following standard IV assumptions:

(A1) Random Assignment of Z: $Z \perp (Y(1), Y(0), D(1), D(0)) \mid X$

(A2) Exclusion Restriction: $Y(z,D(z)) = Y(D(z))$ for all $z$

This also implies:

$$

Y(D(1)) = Y(1)D(1) + Y(0)(1-D(1)) \\

Y(D(0)) = Y(1)D(0) + Y(0)(1-D(0))

$$

(A3) Relevance: $E[D(1)] \neq E[D(0)]$

Additionally we impose the assumption of monotonicity and rule out defiers:

(A4) Monotonicity: $P(D(1) \geq D(0)) = 1$

(A5) Consistency: $D = D(Z)$ and $Y = Y(z,D(z)) \stackrel{\text{(A2)}}{=} Y(D(z))$

First we need to show, that $\tau_{LATT}$ that is defined as $\tau_{LATT} = E[Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1]$ can be written as the ratio of the ATT of the instrument on the outcome and the ATT of the instrument on the treatment:

$$\tau_{LATT} = \frac{E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1]} {E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1]}$$

We start this proof with the numerator of the second term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A2)}}{=} E[ Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& = E[ Y(1)D(1) + Y(0)(1-D(1)) - (Y(1)D(0) + Y(0)(1-D(0))) \mid D=1] \\
&= E[Y(1)D(1) + Y(0) - Y(0)D(1) - Y(1)D(0) - Y(0) + Y(0)D(0) \mid D=1] \\
& = E[ Y(1)(D(1)-D(0)) + Y(0)(D(0)-D(1)) \mid D=1] \\
& = E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We can divide this based on four diffeerent compliance types:

1.  Always Takers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=1 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 0$
2.  Never Takers: $D(1)=0, D(0)=0 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 0$
3.  Compliers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=0 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 1$
4.  Defiers: $D(1)=0, D(0)=1 \implies D(1) - D(0) = -1$

Under our monotonicity assumption (A4) we can rule out defiers. Formally this implies that $P(D(1) - D(0) = -1 ) = P(Defiers) = P(Defiers \mid D=1) = 0$.

Also, when we condition on D = 1, never takers are not included in this group, since they never take the treatment regardless of the instrument value. Thus, when we condition on D = 1, the only compliance types that remain are always takers and compliers. Formally, $P(Never Takers | D=1) = 0$.

We can rewite our previous equation using the law of iterated expectations and conditioning on the compliance types:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Always Takers}] \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Never Takers}] \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0))(D(1)-D(0)) \mid D=1, \text{Defiers}] \cdot P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1) \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A4)}}{=} 0 \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + 0 \cdot P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[ (Y(1)-Y(0)) \cdot 1 \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + (-1) \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& = E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& = E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] \cdot P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We have show the equivalence of:

$$
E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1] = E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1)
$$

Now we can rearrange this to express $\tau_{LATT}$:

$$
E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] = \frac{E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1]}{P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1)}
$$

Now we show that the denominator can be written as $E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Always Takers}] \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Never Takers}] \cdot  \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& + E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Compliers}] \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1, \text{Defiers}] \cdot P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1) \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A4)}}{=} 0 \cdot P(\text{Always Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + 0 \cdot P(\text{Never Takers} \mid D=1) \\
& + 1 \cdot P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& + (-1) \cdot \underset{\textstyle=0}{\underbrace{P(\text{Defiers} \mid D=1)}} \\
& = P(\text{Compliers} \mid D=1) \\
& = P(D(0)=0, D(1)=1 \mid D=1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Plugging this back into our previous equation we get:

$$
E[ Y(1)-Y(0) \mid D(0)=0, D(1)=1, D=1] = \frac{E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1]}{E[D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1]} \quad \blacksquare
$$
Which we wanted to show.

We will now use this result to show identification of $\tau_{LATT}$ under our IV assumptions (A1)-(A5).

Starting with the numerator:

We will proced tho show that the numerator is identifed by follwing AIPW structure, which we can split in regression and correction part:

$$\frac{1}{P(D=1)} E \left[ Z Y - \left( \underset{\text{Regression Part}}{\underbrace{P(Z=1|X) E[Y|X, Z=0]}} + \underset{\text{Correction Part}}{\underbrace{\frac{P(Z=1|X)(1-Z)(Y - E[Y|X, Z=0])}{1 - P(Z=1|X)}}} \right) \right]$$


We start by basic rewriting.

We have already shown that we can use the exclusion restriction (A2) to rewrite the numerator as:

$$
E[ Y(1,D(1)) - Y(0,D(0)) \mid D=1] = E[ Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) \mid D=1]
$$

By the definition of conditional expectation we can write this as:

$$
E[ Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) \mid D=1 ] = \frac{E[ (Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0))) \cdot D ]}{P(D=1)}
$$

We can again identify 4 types:

1. Always Takers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=1 \implies Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) = 0$
2. Never Takers: $D(1)=0, D(0)=0 \implies Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) = 0$
3. Compliers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=0 \implies Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) = Y(1) - Y(0)$
4. Defiers: Ruled out by Montonicity (A4)

So the term $E[ (Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0))) \cdot D ]$ is non-zero only for compliers. We also already know from above that for complies $D=Z$. Therefore we can  replace $D$ in the numerator expectation:

$$E[ (Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0))) \cdot D ] = E[ (Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0))) \cdot Z ]$$

Now let's focus on the regression part:

$$E[ P(Z=1|X) E[Y|X, Z=0] ]$$

Using Consistency and (A1 - $Z \perp Y(D(0)) \mid X$) we can write:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[ P(Z=1|X) E[Y|X, Z=0] ] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A5)}}{=} E[ P(Z=1|X) E[Y(D(0))|X,Z=0] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A1)}}{=} E[ P(Z=1|X) E[YD(0)|X] ] \\
& = E[ E[Z|X] E[Y(D(0))|X] ] \\
& = E[ E[Z  Y(D(0))|X] ] \\
& = E[ Z Y(D(0)) ]
\end{aligned}
$$

Now let's look at the correction part:

$$E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (Y - E[Y|X, Z=0]) \right]$$

By (A1) Random Assignment and (A2) Exclusion and (A5) Consistency: 

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[Y|X, Z=0] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A5&A2)}}{=} E[Y(D(0))|X, Z=0] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A1)}}{=} E[Y(D(0))|X] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Let's plug this in the correction part and apply the LIE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (Y - E[Y|X, Z=0]) \right] \\
& = E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (Y - E[Y(D(0))|X]) \right] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E \left[ E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (Y - E[Y(D(0))|X]) \mid X \right] \right] \\
& = E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} E \left[ (1-Z) (Y - E[Y(D(0))|X]) \mid X \right] \right] \\
\end{aligned}
$$
Focus on the inner part and apply the LIE again:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E \left[ (1-Z) (Y - E[Y(D(0))|X]) \mid X \right] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} \ E[(1-1)(Y-E[Y(D(0))|X]) \mid X, Z=1] \cdot P(Z=1 \mid X) \\
& + E[(1-0)(Y-E[Y(D(0))|X]) \mid X, Z=0] \cdot P(Z=0 \mid X) \\
& =  E[(Y-E[Y(D(0))|X]) \mid X, Z=0] \cdot P(Z=0 \mid X) \\
& = (1 - P(Z=1|X)) \cdot (E[Y \mid X, Z=0] - E[Y(D(0))|X]) \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A1&A5&A2)}}{=} (1 - P(Z=1|X)) \cdot ( E[Y(D(0)) \mid X] - E[Y(D(0))|X]) \\
& = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$
Now we look at the full AIPW representation of the numerator again, which we wrote as:

$$E \left[ Z Y - \left( \text{Regression Part} + \text{Correction Part} \right) \right]$$

We have shown that the regression part is equal to $E[ Z Y(D(0)) ]$ and the correction part is equal to 0. Additionally since $Z=1$ under Consistency $D=D(1)$ and  $Y = Y(D(1))$. Then the first part becomes:

$$E[ZY] = E[Z Y(D(1))]$$

Putting everything together we and remebering our first result when rewriting $ \frac{E[ Z (Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0))) ]}{P(D=1)} = E[ Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) \mid D=1 ] $ we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{P(D=1)} E \left[ Z Y - \left( \text{Regression Part} + \text{Correction Part} \right) \right] \\
& = \frac{1}{P(D=1)} E[Z Y(D(1))] - E[ Z Y(D(0)) ] \\
& = \frac{E[ Z (Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0))) ]}{P(D=1)} \\
& = E[ Y(D(1)) - Y(D(0)) \mid D=1 ] \\ 
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore we have shown that the numerator is identified by the AIPW structure above.

Let's do the same for the denominator:

We will proceed tho show that the denominator is identified by the same AIPW structure:

$$\frac{1}{P(D=1)} E \left[ Z D - \left( \underset{\text{Regression Part}}{\underbrace{P(Z=1|X) E[D|X, Z=0]}} + \underset{\text{Correction Part}}{\underbrace{\frac{P(Z=1|X)(1-Z)(D - E[D|X, Z=0])}{1 - P(Z=1|X)}}} \right) \right]$$

Let's rewrite again:

$$E[ D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1]$$

By the definition of conditional expectation we can write this as:
$$E[ D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1 ] = \frac{E[ (D(1) - D(0)) \cdot D ]}{P(D=1)}$$
We can again identify 4 types:

1. Always Takers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=1 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 0$
2. Never Takers: $D(1)=0, D(0)=0 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 0$
3.Compliers: $D(1)=1, D(0)=0 \implies D(1) - D(0) = 1$
4.Defiers: Ruled out by Montonicity (A4)

So the term $E[ (D(1) - D(0)) \cdot D ]$ is non-zero only for compliers. We also already know from above that for complies $D=Z$. Therefore we can  replace $D$ (the multiplier) in the denominator expectation:

$$E[ (D(1) - D(0)) \cdot D ] = E[ (D(1) - D(0)) \cdot Z ]$$
Now let's focus on the regression part:

$$E[ P(Z=1|X) E[D|X, Z=0] ]$$

Using Consistency and (A1 - $Z \perp D(0) \mid X$) we can write:

$$\begin{aligned}
& E[ P(Z=1|X) E[D|X, Z=0] ] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A5)}}{=} E[ P(Z=1|X) E[D(0)|X,Z=0] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A1)}}{=} E[ P(Z=1|X) E[D(0)|X] ] \\
& = E[ E[Z|X] E[D(0)|X] ] \\
& = E[ E[Z  D(0)|X] ] \\
& = E[ Z D(0) ]
\end{aligned}$$

Now let's look at the correction part:
$$E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (D - E[D|X, Z=0]) \right]$$

By (A1) Random Assignment and (A5) Consistency:

$$\begin{aligned}
& E[D|X, Z=0] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A5)}}{=} E[D(0)|X, Z=0] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A1)}}{=} E[D(0)|X] \\
\end{aligned}$$

Let's plug this in the correction part and apply the LIE:

$$\begin{aligned}
& E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (D - E[D|X, Z=0]) \right] \\
& = E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (D - E[D(0)|X]) \right] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} E \left[ E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} (1-Z) (D - E[D(0)|X]) \mid X \right] \right] \\
& = E \left[ \frac{P(Z=1|X)}{1 - P(Z=1|X)} E \left[ (1-Z) (D - E[D(0)|X]) \mid X \right] \right] \\
\end{aligned}$$

Focus on the inner part and apply the LIE again:

$$\begin{aligned}
& E \left[ (1-Z) (D - E[D(0)|X]) \mid X \right] \\
& \stackrel{\text{(LIE)}}{=} \ E[(1-1)(D-E[D(0)|X]) \mid X, Z=1] \cdot P(Z=1 \mid X) \\
& + E[(1-0)(D-E[D(0)|X]) \mid X, Z=0] \cdot P(Z=0 \mid X) \\
& =  E[(D-E[D(0)|X]) \mid X, Z=0] \cdot P(Z=0 \mid X) \\
& = (1 - P(Z=1|X)) \cdot (E[D \mid X, Z=0] - E[D(0)|X]) \\
& \stackrel{\text{(A1&A5)}}{=} (1 - P(Z=1|X)) \cdot ( E[D(0) \mid X] - E[D(0)|X]) \\
& = 0 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Now we look at the full AIPW representation of the denominator again, which we wrote as:

$$E \left[ Z D - \left( \text{Regression Part} + \text{Correction Part} \right) \right]$$
We have shown that the regression part is equal to $E[ Z D(0) ]$ and the correction part is equal to 0.

Additionally since $Z=1$ under Consistency $D=D(1)$. 

Then the first part becomes:

$$E[ZD] = E[Z D(1)]$$

Putting everything together we and remebering our first result when rewriting $\frac{E[ Z (D(1) - D(0)) ]}{P(D=1)} = E[ D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1 ]$ we get:

$$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{P(D=1)} E \left[ Z D - \left( \text{Regression Part} + \text{Correction Part} \right) \right] \\
& = \frac{1}{P(D=1)} E[Z D(1)] - E[ Z D(0) ] \\
& = \frac{E[ Z (D(1) - D(0)) ]}{P(D=1)} \\
& = E[ D(1) - D(0) \mid D=1 ] \\ 
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore we have shown that the denominator is identified by the AIPW structure above.

We have succesfully shown that both the numerator and denominator of the LATT are identified by the AIPW-ATT structure under our IV assumptions (A1)-(A5). Thus the LATT itself is identified as the ratio of the two identified AIPW-ATT ratios. 


### 2.3 Influence function derivation

We have shown in the previous section that the LATT can be expressed as a ratio of two ATTs. As we know the Neyman-orthogonal score for the ATT, we can use the chain rule to derive an influence function for the LATT.

The Neyman orthogonal score of the ATT is given by:

$$
\psi_{ATT}(O;\theta;\eta) = \underset{\textstyle=\tilde{Y}_{ATT}}{\underbrace{ \frac{D}{e}(Y - m(0,X)) - \frac{(1-D)e(X)}{e(1-e(X))}(Y - m(0,X))}}  - \tau_{ATT} \frac{W}{e}
$$

With $O=(D,X,Y)$, $\theta = \tau_{ATT}$, $\eta = (m(0,X), e(X), e)$ and the two linear score components $\psi_a(O;\eta) = (-1)\frac{D}{e}$ and $\psi_b(O;\eta) = \tilde{Y}_{ATT}$.

$m(0,X)$ is the regression function of $Y$ given $D=0$ and $X$, $e(X)$ is the propensity score of $D$ given $X$ and $e$ is the unconditional probability of treatment.

Let's tailor this score function now to the two specific ATTs we have.

Let's start with the numerator, which is the ATT of the instrument on the outcome. Therefore we simply need to replace $D$ with $Z$ and all nuisance parameters respectively:

$$
\psi_{ATT}^{Y}(O;\theta;\eta)
= \underset{\textstyle=\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z}}{\underbrace{ \frac{Z}{h}(Y - m_{Z}(0,X)) - \frac{(1-Z)(h(X))}{h(1-h(X))}(Y - m_{Z}(0,X))}}  - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}
$$

, where $O=(Z,X,Y)$, $\theta = \tau_{ATT}^{Y}$, $\eta = (m_{Z}(0,X), h(X), h)$ and the two linear score components $\psi_a^{Y}(O;\eta) = (-1)\frac{Z}{h}$ and $\psi_b^{Y}(O;\eta) = \tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z}$.

The nuisance parameters are now defined as follows: $m_{Z}(0,X)$ is the regression function of $Y$ given $Z=0$ and $X$, $h(X)$ is the propensity score of $Z$ given $X$ and $h$ is the unconditional probability of $Z$.

Let's proceed with the denominator, which is the ATT of the instrument on the treatment:

We define $O=(Z,X,D)$, $\theta = \tau_{ATT}^{D}$, $\eta = (e_{Z}(0,X), h(X), h)$ and the two linear score components $\psi_a^{D}(O;\eta) = (-1)\frac{Z}{h}$ and $\psi_b^{D}(O;\eta) = \tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z}$.

The complete score function for the ATT of the instrument on the treatment is then given by:

$$
\psi_{ATT}^{D}(O;\theta;\eta) = \underset{\textstyle=\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z}}{\underbrace{ \frac{Z}{h}(D - e_{Z}(0,X)) - \frac{(1-Z)(h(X))}{h(1-h(X))}(D - e_{Z}(0,X))}}  - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}
$$

Now that we have defined the two score functions for the numerator and denominator, we can derive the two influence functions $\Psi_{ATT}^{Y}$ and $\Psi_{ATT}^{D}$ using the linear components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Psi_{ATT}^{Y} = - \mathbb{E}[\psi_a^{Y}]^{-1} \cdot \psi_{ATT}^{Y} \\
& = - \left(\mathbb{E}\left[-\frac{Z}{h}\right]\right)^{-1} \cdot \left(\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = - \left(-\frac{\mathbb{E}[Z]}{h}\right)^{-1} \cdot \left(\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = - \left(-\frac{h}{h}\right)^{-1} \left(\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = - \left(-1\right)^{-1} \left(\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = \tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Subsequently for the ATT of the instrument on the treatment we have:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Psi_{ATT}^{D} = - \mathbb{E}[\psi_a^{D}]^{-1} \cdot \psi_{ATT}^{D} \\
& = - \left(\mathbb{E}\left[-\frac{Z}{h}\right]\right)^{-1} \cdot \left(\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = - \left(-\frac{\mathbb{E}[Z]}{h}\right)^{-1} \cdot \left(\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = - \left(-\frac{h}{h}\right)^{-1} \left(\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = - \left(-1\right)^{-1} \left(\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = \tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Our LATT parameter is a ratio of the two ATTs:

$$
\tau_{LATT} = \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}}
$$

Using the chain rule we can derive the influence function for the LATT as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Psi_{LATT} = \frac{\partial \tau_{LATT}}{\partial \tau_{ATT}^{Y}} \Psi_{ATT}^{Y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{LATT}}{\partial \tau_{ATT}^{D}} \Psi_{ATT}^{D} \\
& = \frac{\Psi_{ATT}^{Y}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} - \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{(\tau_{ATT}^{D})^2} \Psi_{ATT}^{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now we plug in the two ATT influence functions we have derived before using our linear components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Psi_{LATT} = \frac{\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} - \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{(\tau_{ATT}^{D})^2} \left(\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}\right) \\
& = \frac{\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} - \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} \frac{Z}{h} - \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{(\tau_{ATT}^{D})^2} \tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \left( \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{(\tau_{ATT}^{D})^2} \right) \left( - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h} \right) \\
& = \frac{\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} - \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} \frac{Z}{h} - \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{(\tau_{ATT}^{D})^2} \tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} + \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} \frac{Z}{h} \\
& = \frac{\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} - \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{(\tau_{ATT}^{D})^2} \tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We can write this in a more compact form by substituting back the definition of $\tau_{LATT}$ and factoring out $\frac{1}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Psi_{LATT} = \frac{1}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} \left( \tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{LATT} \cdot \tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} \right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is our final influence function for the LATT. We can observe that the final version simplifies to a scaled type of pseudo outcome on pseudo outcome regression, which looks very familiar.

Before we can apply the Double Machine Learning framework we additionally need to derive the new empirical moment condition of $\tau_{LATT}$ and solve it.

Before looking at the ratio of the two ATT Neyman-orthogonal score let's first

We have shown that $\tau_{LATT}$ can be expressed by the ratio of two ATTs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \tau_{LATT} = \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} \\
& = \frac{E[\psi_{ATT}^{Y}]}{E[\psi_{ATT}^{D}]} \\
& \implies E[\psi_{ATT}^{Y} - \tau_{LATT} \psi_{ATT}^{D}] = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Now let's look at this moment condition in more detail:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[\psi_{ATT}^{Y} - \tau_{LATT} \cdot \psi_{ATT}^{D}] = 0 \\
& E[(\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}) - \tau_{LATT} \cdot (\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h})] = 0 \\
& E[\underset{Residual \ Part}{\underbrace{(\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{LATT}\cdot\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z})}} + \underset{Centering \ Part}{\underbrace{(- \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h} + \tau_{LATT}\cdot\tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h})}}] = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Let's zoom in on the centering part, plug in the definition of $\tau_{LATT}$ and show that its expectation is equal to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E[- \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h} + \tau_{LATT}\cdot\tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}] \\
& = E[- \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h} + \frac{\tau_{ATT}^{Y}}{\tau_{ATT}^{D}} \cdot\tau_{ATT}^{D} \frac{Z}{h}] \\
& = E[- \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h} + \tau_{ATT}^{Y} \frac{Z}{h}] \\
& = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the moment condition simplifies to:

$$
E[\tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z} - \tau_{LATT}\cdot\tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z}] = 0
$$

With the two linear score components $\psi_a(O; \eta) = (-1) \tilde{D}_{ATT}^{Z}$ and $\psi_b(O; \eta) = \tilde{Y}_{ATT}^{Z}$.

Now that we have derived the influence function and the empirical Neyman-orthogonal moment condition for our new parameter $\tau_{LATT}$, we can use following procedure to estimate $\hat\tau_{LATT}$ using the Double Machine Learning framework:

1.  Estimate the nuisance parameters $m_{Z}(0,X)$, $e_{Z}(0,X)$, $h(X)$ and $h$ with cross-fitted high quality machine learning methods.

2.  Estimate the target parameter $\tau_{LATT}$ by solving the empirical moment condition:

$$
\hat\tau_{LATT} = - \frac{\sum_{i} \psi_b(O_i; \hat{\eta}_i)}{\sum_{i} \psi_a(O_i; \hat{\eta}_i)}
$$

Plugging our derived linear scores in:

$$
\hat\tau_{LATT} = - \frac{\sum_{i} \tilde{Y}_{ATT,i}^{Z}}{\sum_{i} (-1) \tilde{D}_{ATT,i}^{Z}} = \frac{\sum_{i} \tilde{Y}_{ATT,i}^{Z}}{\sum_{i} \tilde{D}_{ATT,i}^{Z}}
$$

3.  Estimate standard errors using the derived influence function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \hat{\sigma}_{LATT} = \operatorname{Var}(\Psi_{LATT}) \\
& \implies \widehat{\operatorname{se}}(\hat{\tau}_{LATT}) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{LATT}}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$
